perturbation.original_text: Neither of us will use nor disclose the other's c onfidential information for three years after we receive it. The three - year time period does not apply if applicable law requires a longer period.
perturbation.changed_text: Neither of us will use nor disclose the other's confidential information for six months after we receive it. The six-month time period does not apply if applicable law requires a shorter period.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the confidentiality period to six months could be insufficient to protect trade secrets under Florida law, potentially leading to irreparable harm.
perturbation.law_explanation: Florida's Uniform Trade Secrets Act does not define a fixed period for trade secret protection, but a six-month period may be deemed unreasonably short, especially given the nature of the confidential information.
perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the confidentiality period from three years to six months. The perturbation explanation states that this reduction could be insufficient to protect trade secrets under Florida law, potentially leading to irreparable harm. The Law Explanation supports this, stating that a six-month period may be deemed unreasonably short. This aligns with the 'Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction' definition because the contract sets a deadline that could violate state law regarding trade secret protection, making the contractual terms unenforceable or illegal.

perturbation.original_text: During the term of the agreement and for a period of twelve (12) months thereafter, each party agrees not to solicit or recruit any employee of each other without the prior written consent of that party.
perturbation.changed_text: During the term of the agreement, each party agrees not to solicit or recruit any employee of each other without the prior written consent of that party. This restriction will not apply after the agreement terminates.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the 12-month non-solicitation period post-termination may violate Florida Statute regarding reasonable restrictions to protect legitimate business interests.
perturbation.law_explanation: Florida Statute 542.335 permits reasonable restrictions on solicitation of employees for a defined period after termination to protect legitimate business interests. Eliminating this post-termination restriction might be deemed unreasonable and thus unenforceable if it fails to protect established customer relationships and trade secrets.
perturbation.justification: The changed text removes the 12-month non-solicitation period post-termination, potentially conflicting with Florida Statute 542.335, which permits reasonable restrictions on solicitation of employees after termination to protect legitimate business interests. The absence of scraped law snippets doesn't negate the potential contradiction, as the Perturbation and Law Explanations both explicitly point to a conflict with Florida law regarding reasonable non-solicitation periods, making the change a clear violation by removing a potentially enforceable protection.

perturbation.original_text: The laws of the State of Florida govern this agreement. If federal jurisdiction exists, we each consent to exclusive jurisdiction and venue in the federal courts in Broward County, Florida . If not, we each consent to exclusive jurisdiction and venue in the Superior Court of Broward County, Florida .
perturbation.changed_text: The laws of the State of Delaware govern this agreement. We each consent to exclusive jurisdiction and venue in the courts of California.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the governing law and venue to California despite the LLC being based and registered in Florida is contradictory to Florida's established business and jurisdictional laws.
perturbation.law_explanation: Florida Statute § 605.0112 establishes that internal affairs of a Florida LLC are governed by Florida law. Selecting Delaware law and California courts could be seen as an attempt to circumvent Florida law governing LLC operations.

perturbation.justification: The changed text contradicts Florida law by selecting Delaware law and California courts for a Florida-based LLC. The Law Explanation states that Florida Statute § 605.0112 establishes that the internal affairs of a Florida LLC are governed by Florida law, which the changed text appears to circumvent. The original text specified Florida law, but the change specifies Delaware law and California courts, creating an inconsistency as defined by the provided perturbation definition.

perturbation.original_text: Each of us may disclose the other's confidential information if required to comply with a court order or ot her government demand that has the force of law. Before doing so, each of us must seek th e highest level o f protection available and, when possible, give the other enough prior notice to provide a reasonab le chance to seek a protective order.
perturbation.changed_text: Each of us may disclose the other's confidential information if required to comply with a court order or other government demand. No prior notice is required.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the requirement to seek protection and provide prior notice before disclosing confidential information under legal demand contradicts established legal practices for protecting trade secrets and confidential information, potentially violating fiduciary duties.
perturbation.law_explanation: Federal Rule of Civil Procedure 45(d)(3)(B) provides protections for those subject to subpoenas, including the ability to object to the disclosure of privileged or protected matter. Removing the requirement for prior notice inhibits the opportunity to seek protection against disclosure.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Rule 45. Subpoena | Federal Rules of Civil Procedure | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Rule 45. Subpoena (a) In General. (1) Form and Contents. (A) Requirements—In General. Every subpoena must: (i) state the court from which it issued; (ii) state the title of the action and its civil-action number; (iii) command each person to whom it is directed to do the following at a specified time and place: attend and testify; produce designated documents, electronically stored information, or tangible things in that person's possession, custody, or control; or permit the inspection of premises; and (iv) set out the text of Rule 45(d) and (e). (B) Command to Attend a Deposition—Notice of the Recording Method. A subpoena commanding attendance at a deposition must state the method for recording the testimony. (C) Combining or Separating a Command to Produce or to Permit Inspection; Specifying the Form for Electronically Stored Information. A command to produce documents, electronically stored information, or tangible things or to permit the inspection of premises may be included in a subpoena commanding attendance at a deposition, hearing, or trial, or may be set out in a separate subpoena. A subpoena may specify the form or forms in which electronically stored information is to be produced. (D) Command to Produce; Included Obligations. A command in a subpoena to produce documents, electronically stored information, or tangible things requires the responding person to permit inspection, copying, testing, or sampling of the materials. (2) Issuing Court. A subpoena must issue from the court where the action is pending. (3) Issued by Whom. The clerk must issue a subpoena, signed but otherwise in blank, to a party who requests it. That party must complete it before service. An attorney also may issue and sign a subpoena if the attorney is authorized to practice in the issuing court. (4) Notice to Other Parties Before Service. If th

Secure .gov websites use HTTPS A lock ( Lock Locked padlock icon ) or https:// means you’ve safely connected to the .gov website. Share sensitive information only on official, secure websites. Federal Rules of Civil Procedure The Federal Rules of Civil Procedure govern civil proceedings in the United States district courts. The purpose of the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure is "to secure the just, speedy, and inexpensive determination of every action and proceeding." Fed. R. Civ. P. 1. The rules were first adopted by order of the Supreme Court on December 20, 1937, transmitted to Congress on January 3, 1938, and effective September 16, 1938. The Civil Rules were last amended in 2024. perturbation.justification: The original contract required seeking the highest level of protection and providing prior notice before disclosing confidential information under legal demand. The changed contract removes the prior notice requirement. The Law Explanation references Federal Rule of Civil Procedure 45(d)(3)(B), which protects against disclosure of privileged or protected matter. By removing the prior notice, the changed contract inhibits the opportunity to seek protection against disclosure, directly contradicting the intent of the law. Scraped Law Snippet 1 explicitly refers to Rule 45, mentioning the requirements and contents of a subpoena, indirectly suggesting the importance of due process and the opportunity to object. The removal of prior notice undermines this process, creating a legal contradiction as it removes a safeguard implicitly supported by Rule 45.

perturbation.original_text: Either of us may terminate this agreement for any reaso n by providing the other with 60 days' advance written notice.
perturbation.changed_text: Either of us may terminate this agreement for any reason by providing the other with 10 days' advance written notice.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the termination notice period to 10 days may not provide sufficient time to wind down projects or protect confidential information, potentially conflicting with the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing under Florida contract law.
perturbation.law_explanation: Florida Statutes § 671.203 states that 'Every contract or duty within this code imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance or enforcement.' A 10-day notice period may be deemed insufficient in some circumstances, violating the implied duty of good faith, especially where significant reliance or ongoing projects exist.

F.S. 671.203 671.203 Obligation of good faith. — Every contract or duty within this code imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement. History. — s. 1, ch. 65-254; s. 10, ch. 2007-134. Note. — s. 1-203, U.C.C.; supersedes ss. 678.54, 614.02(11).
perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the termination notice period from 60 days to 10 days. The Law Explanation states that Florida Statutes § 671.203 imposes an obligation of good faith in the performance of contracts. The Perturbation Explanation suggests that a 10-day notice period may be insufficient to wind down projects or protect confidential information, potentially conflicting with the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing F.S. 671.203, confirms that 'Every contract or duty within this code imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement.' Reducing the notice period to 10 days could be considered a violation of this duty, especially if it doesn't allow sufficient time to protect the other party's interests, creating a contradiction.
